straight_beat.name: Delmar Fontenot
straight_beat.column:
The Astros lost again. Not in a way that’s shocking, but in a way that feels like a pattern now. Yesterday’s 2-6 drubbing against Seattle wasn’t just another loss—it was a reminder of how the team’s pitching staff is struggling to hold serve. Altuve and Alvarez did their part, sure, but the rest of the lineup looked like a group of guys who’ve forgotten how to hit for power. That’s the tape measure of it.  

Let’s start with the starters. Framber Valdez, who’s been a stabilizing force, lasted just five innings. He allowed three runs, walked two, and looked like he was trying to pitch through a fog machine. The problem isn’t just Valdez—it’s the whole rotation. They’ve been inconsistent, and the deeper you dig, the more you realize they’re not just missing strikeouts; they’re missing the ability to command their stuff. The pitch counts on the chalkboard in my garage look like a spreadsheet for a college football game. Some guys are throwing 100 pitches, others 60. There’s no rhythm, no plan.  

The bullpen, meanwhile, is acting like a group of guys who

optimist.name: Luz Barquero
optimist.column:
The Astros lost yesterday, but here’s the thing: they’re still in this thing. You hear that? Still in this thing. That’s the sound of a team that’s not just surviving but figuring out how to thrive. Sure, the Mariners handed them a 6-2 defeat, but let’s not let that number cloud the fact that Houston’s got a bunch of guys out there swinging for the fences, and some of them are starting to hit them.  

Take Bregman, for instance. He’s been the heartbeat of this offense, and yesterday? He didn’t strike out. He didn’t walk. He took a few pitches, stayed in the zone, and when the count got full, he swung and made it count. One at-bat away from flipping April into June, as I told my husband last night. He just rolled his eyes, but I know he’s listening. Bregman’s swing is a pendulum—sometimes it’s swinging toward the middle, sometimes it’s swinging toward the fences. And when it’s swinging toward the fences? That’s when the magic happens.  

Then there’s Correa. The kid’s got a cannon for an arm, and yesterday, he made sure the Mariners knew it. A couple of strikeouts, a couple of walks, but when he needed to, he delivered. And let’s not forget Altuve. The man’s a machine, and even when the numbers don’t show it, he’s out there grinding. You

pessimist.name: Coop Yarbrough
pessimist.column:
The Astros lost again. Not in a way that feels inevitable, but in a way that feels… familiar. Another game where they clawed back from a deficit, only to watch the Mariners’ bats carve up their bullpen like a Thanksgiving turkey. Another loss that lands them at 6-11, 3.5 games behind the Angels in a division that’s already decided to take the easy way out. You’d think a team with this many chances to win would have figured out by now that the universe has a cruel sense of humor when it comes to their success.  

The numbers don’t lie. They’re not lying. The Astros are winning games they shouldn’t, losing games they should
